proof_stages.label: Class-number formula and exceptional-zero obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Dirichlet characters and the class-number formula, explaining that effective lower bounds for L(1,chi_D) are obstructed precisely by possible real zeros of Dirichlet L-functions very close to s=1, while Landau's and Siegel's lower bounds are ineffective.

proof_stages.label: Landscape of conditional consequences of Landau--Siegel zeros
proof_stages.summary: The speaker surveys how arguments split according to the presence or absence of exceptional zeros, listing effective class-number consequences, 'illusory' prime-distribution results, pair-correlation effects, and an earlier hypothesis-H result ruling out sequences of such zeros under GRH except for real zeros.

proof_stages.label: Euler-product mechanism forcing chi to mimic the Möbius function
proof_stages.summary: Using the Euler product and the convolution sum of 1*chi, the talk explains that if L(1,chi) is very small then most relevant primes have chi(p)=-1, so chi behaves like the Möbius function on many squarefree integers.

proof_stages.label: Dirichlet central-value nonvanishing problem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the family problem for central values L(1/2,psi) and their derivatives over characters modulo q, recalls known unconditional and GRH-level nonvanishing proportions, and asks whether an exceptional zero improves the rate toward full nonvanishing.

proof_stages.label: Conditional Dirichlet-family theorem from one strong exceptional zero
proof_stages.summary: Under a sufficiently strong exceptional character chi_D and for primes q in a range between large powers of D, the speaker states joint work with Hung Bui and Kyle Pratt giving at least 50 percent nonvanishing of L(1/2,psi) and showing that almost all such Dirichlet L-functions have at most a simple central zero.

proof_stages.label: Weight-two newforms, parity, and the 50-percent barrier
proof_stages.summary: The talk then shifts to weight-two newforms of prime level, explains parity-forced central vanishing and analytic rank, recalls known rank-distribution bounds and Brumer--Murty-type expectations, and describes earlier moment work showing why beating the natural 50-percent threshold would threaten Landau--Siegel zeros.

proof_stages.label: Conditional modular-form rank theorem in the exceptional-zero range
proof_stages.summary: The final theorem states that, assuming one strong Landau--Siegel zero and taking q in the resulting finite power range, almost all relevant modular forms have analytic rank bounded by the parity-minimal thresholds, in particular zero percent have rank at least three and almost all odd forms have rank exactly one.
